copd: A long-term lung disease that makes it harder to breathe. Airways become inflamed and airflow is limited.
spirometry: A breathing test that measures how much air you can breathe in and out and how fast you can exhale.
fev1: The amount of air you can forcefully breathe out in the first second of a breathing test.
fvc: The total amount of air you can breathe out after taking a deep breath.
salbutamol: A quick-relief inhaler medicine that opens the airways (often called albuterol).
nebulization: A treatment that turns liquid medicine into a fine mist you breathe in through a mask or mouthpiece.
prednisolone: A steroid medicine that reduces swelling and irritation in the lungs and airways.
amoxicillin: An antibiotic used to treat bacterial infections.
exacerbation: A sudden worsening of symptoms compared to your usual day-to-day state.
corticosteroid: A type of medicine that calms inflammation in the body, including the lungs.
spo2: A measure of how much oxygen is in your blood, checked with a small sensor on your finger.
pulmonology: The medical specialty focused on lungs and breathing problems.
dyspnea: The feeling of being short of breath or having trouble catching your breath.
myocardial infarction: A heart attack — when blood flow to part of the heart is blocked.
hypertension: High blood pressure — the force of blood against artery walls is higher than it should be.
mi: Short for myocardial infarction — commonly called a heart attack.
ejection fraction: A percentage that shows how well the heart pumps blood out with each beat.
diuretic: A "water pill" that helps your body get rid of extra fluid through urine.
lisinopril: A blood pressure medicine in the ACE inhibitor family.
furosemide: A strong diuretic used to reduce fluid buildup, including in heart failure.
